perturbation.original_text: 4. Return of Confidential Information. If activity in respect of the Transaction shall cease without the Transaction being consummated, then, promptly after the written request of the Disclosing Party, the Receiving Party shall return to the Disclosing Party all Confidential Information which is in tangible form and which is then in its possession (or in the possession of any of its officers, directors or employees).
perturbation.changed_text: 4. Return of Confidential Information. If activity in respect of the Transaction shall cease without the Transaction being consummated, then, after 90 days from the written request of the Disclosing Party, the Receiving Party shall return to the Disclosing Party all Confidential Information which is in tangible form and which is then in its possession (or in the possession of any of its officers, directors or employees).
perturbation.explanation: Delaying the return of confidential information to 90 days after the written request contradicts the requirement to return the information 'promptly'. Prompt action is a generally expected standard in contracts, but the change does not respect the 'promptly' term.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Breach of Contract - Implied Duty of Good Faith and Fair Dealing
perturbation.law_explanation: Minnesota law implies a duty of good faith and fair dealing in the performance of contracts. Unreasonably delaying the return of confidential information, when the contract originally stipulated 'promptly', could be interpreted as a breach of this duty, as it frustrates the purpose of the agreement and the disclosing party's ability to protect its sensitive data. The modified text contradicts the implied duty to act in good faith.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: Sec. 336.1-304 MN Statutes Skip to main content Skip to office menu Skip to footer Minnesota Legislature Advanced Search Search Legislature Search Office of the Revisor of Statutes Office of the Revisor of Statutes 2024 Minnesota Statutes TRADE REGULATIONS, CONSUMER PROTECTION Chapter 336 Section 336.1-304 336.1-303 336.1-305 2024 Minnesota Statutes Authenticate PDF Resources Search Minnesota Statutes About Minnesota Statutes 2024 Statutes New, Amended or Repealed 2024 Table of Chapters 2024 Statutes Topics (Index) Chapter 336 Table of Sections Full Chapter Text Version List Section 336.1-304 Version List Recent History 2004 336.1-304 New 2004 c 162 art 1 s 19 336.1-304 OBLIGATION OF GOOD FAITH. Every contract or duty within the Uniform Commercial Code imposes an obligation of good faith in its performance and enforcement. History: 2004 c 162 art 1 s 19 Official Publication of the State of Minnesota Revisor of Statutes
perturbation.justification: The modification from 'promptly' to '90 days' introduces a contradiction concerning the duty of good faith. The original contract requires prompt action, while the modified contract allows for a delay of 90 days, potentially frustrating the disclosing party's ability to protect sensitive data. This delay contradicts the implied duty to act in good faith. Scraped Law Snippet 1, referencing MN Statute 336.1-304, states that 'Every contract or duty within the Uniform Commercial Code imposes an obligation of good faith in its performance and enforcement.' By unreasonably delaying the return of confidential information, the modified contract arguably breaches this obligation.

perturbation.original_text: 5. Term. This Agreement shall continue indefinitely.
perturbation.changed_text: 5. Term. This Agreement shall continue for a period of 1 year.
perturbation.explanation: The initial agreement stated it continues indefinitely. By introducing a definite time-period, it is possible that proprietary information could be compromised if the term ends and information is leaked.
perturbation.law_explanation: While not directly violating the DTSA, limiting the NDA to one year weakens the protection of trade secrets. If trade secrets are misappropriated after the NDA expires, proving a violation becomes more difficult, potentially undermining the intent of the DTSA to protect valuable confidential information. The law ensures liability to extend as long as there is reasonable threat.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: 18 U.S. Code § 1836 - Civil proceedings | U.S. Code | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you Quick search by citation: Title Section Go! 18 U.S. Code § 1836 - Civil proceedings U.S. Code Notes prev | next (a) The Attorney General may, in a civil action, obtain appropriate injunctive relief against any violation of this chapter. (b) Private Civil Actions.— (1) In general.— An owner of a trade secret that is misappropriated may bring a civil action under this subsection if the trade secret is related to a product or service used in, or intended for use in, interstate or foreign commerce. (2) Civil seizure.— (A) In general.— (i) Application.— Based on an affidavit or verified complaint satisfying the requirements of this paragraph, the court may, upon ex parte application but only in extraordinary circumstances, issue an order providing for the seizure of property necessary to prevent the propagation or dissemination of the trade secret that is the subject of the action. (ii) Requirements for issuing order.— The court may not grant an application under clause (i) unless the court finds that it clearly appears from specific facts that— (I) an order issued pursuant to Rule 65 of the Federal Rules of Civil Procedure or another form of equitable relief would be inadequate to achieve the purpose of this paragraph because the party to which the order would be issued would evade, avoid, or otherwise not comply with such an order; (II) an immediate and irreparable injury will occur if such seizure is not ordered; (III) the harm to the applicant of denying the application outweighs the harm to the legitimate interests of the person against whom seizure would be ordered of granting the application and substantially outweighs the harm to any third parties who may be harmed by such seizure; (IV) the applicant is likely to succeed in showing that— (aa) the information is a trade secret ; and (bb) the person against whom seizure would be ordered— (AA
perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: 404 | Berkman Klein Center Skip to the main content Page Cannot Be Found Brave traveler, your journey on our site has brought you to an abrupt dead end. Perhaps you wish to explore one of these other options? About Us Get Involved Events Community Publications Projects & Tools Last updated Mar 27, 2024
perturbation.justification: The original text states the agreement continues indefinitely, while the changed text limits it to one year. The Law Explanation suggests this weakens trade secret protection under the DTSA because proving a violation after the NDA expires becomes more difficult. While the provided snippets do not directly address time limits on trade secret protection, Snippet 1 discusses civil actions for misappropriation of trade secrets (18 U.S. Code § 1836). By limiting the agreement to one year, it creates an inconsistency regarding how long proprietary information is protected, potentially undermining the ability to bring a civil action if misappropriation occurs after the one-year term, establishing a legal contradiction under 'Inconsistencies Legal Contradiction'.

perturbation.original_text: 7. Governing Law. This Agreement shall be a contract under the State of Minnesota and for all purposes shall be governed by and construed and enforced in accordance with the laws of Minnesota, excluding any choice of law provisions.
perturbation.changed_text: 7. Governing Law. This Agreement shall be a contract under the State of California and for all purposes shall be governed by and construed and enforced in accordance with the laws of California, excluding any choice of law provisions.
perturbation.explanation: The contract was originally under Minnesota Law. By switching it to California Law, it could allow for situations where non-compete laws that are standard in Minnesota, could cause breaches under California Law.
perturbation.contradicted_law: California Business and Professions Code Section 16600
perturbation.law_citation: California Business and Professions Code Section 16600
perturbation.law_explanation: California law generally prohibits non-compete agreements, while Minnesota law allows them under certain circumstances. By changing the governing law to California, the enforceability of any non-compete provisions implicitly or explicitly present within the broader business arrangement related to the confidential information covered by the NDA is jeopardized, potentially violating California Business and Professions Code Section 16600.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: California Code, BPC 16600 skip to content home accessibility FAQ feedback sitemap login x Quick Search: Bill Number Bill Keyword Home Bill Information California Law Publications Other Resources My Subscriptions My Favorites California Law >> >> Code Section Code Section Code: Select Code CONS BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Article: Section: Code: Select Code All BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Section: Keyword(s): Code Search Text Search Up^ << Previous Next >> cross-reference chaptered bills PDF | Add To My Favorites Search Phrase: Code Text Business and Professions Code - BPC DIVISION 7. GENERAL BUSINESS REGULATIONS [16000 - 18107] ( Division 7 added by Stats. 1941, Ch. 61. ) PART 2. PRESERVATION AND REGULATION OF COMPETITION [16600 - 17365] ( Part 2 added by Stats. 1941, Ch. 526. ) CHAPTER 1. Contracts in Restraint of Trade [16600 - 16607] ( Chapter 1 added by Stats. 1941, Ch. 526. ) 16600. (a) Except as provided in this chapter, every contract by which anyone is restrained from engaging in a lawful profession, trade, or business of any kind is to that extent void. (b) (1) This section shall be read broadly, in accordance with Edwards v. Arthur Andersen LLP (2008) 44 Cal.4th 937, to void the application of any noncompete agreement in an employment context, or any noncompete clause in an employment contract, no matter how narrowly tailored, that does not satisfy an exception in this chapter. (2) This subdivision does not constitute a change in, but is declaratory of, existing law. (c) This section shall not be limited to contracts where the person being restrained from engaging in a lawful profession, trade, or business is a party to the contract. (Amended by Stats. 2023, Ch. 828, Sec. 1. (AB 1076) Effective January 1, 2024.)
perturbation.justification: The modification of the governing law from Minnesota to California creates a legal contradiction. California Business and Professions Code Section 16600(a), as shown in the scraped law snippet, states that "every contract by which anyone is restrained from engaging in a lawful profession, trade, or business of any kind is to that extent void," with limited exceptions not detailed here. The Perturbation Explanation notes that changing the governing law to California jeopardizes non-compete provisions, which are standard in Minnesota but potentially cause breaches under California law. Therefore, the changed text introduces a situation where clauses enforceable under Minnesota law could be deemed void under California law, directly contradicting BPC 16600(a).

perturbation.original_text: 13. Notices. Any notice required by this Agreement or given in connection with this Agreement, shall be in writing, hand delivered or sent via registered or certified mail, and shall be given to the appropriate party: If to Disclosing Party: Oregon Trail Ethanol Coalition, L.L.C. 102 West 6th Street Box 267 Davenport, Nebraska 68335 Attn: Mark L. Jagels, President With a copy to: Baird Holm Law Firm, LLC 1500 Woodmen Tower Omaha, Nebraska 68102 Attn: Victoria H. Finley GreenWay Consulting, LLC 74 South County Road 22 Morris, Minnesota 56267 If to Receiving Party: ------------------------------------ ------------------------------------ ------------------------------------
perturbation.changed_text: 13. Notices. Any notice required by this Agreement or given in connection with this Agreement, shall be communicated orally, either in person or via telephone, at the discretion of the notifying party. Formal written documentation is explicitly waived to expedite communication.
perturbation.explanation: The original agreement specified written notice via hand delivery or registered/certified mail. Changing this to allow oral notice only and waiving written documentation directly contradicts the Statute of Frauds in most states, including Minnesota and Nebraska, regarding contracts that cannot be performed within one year.
perturbation.law_explanation: Minnesota's Statute of Frauds (and similar statutes in other states) requires certain contracts, including those that cannot be performed within one year, to be in writing and signed by the party against whom enforcement is sought. By eliminating the requirement for written notice and allowing only oral communication, the modified clause makes the agreement potentially unenforceable under the Statute of Frauds, as it removes a key element necessary for establishing a legally binding agreement. Although the original contract does not necessarily violate the Statute of Frauds (since the term was indefinite), the change could violate it if the information is such that it would take longer than one year to be protected.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: Sec. 513.01 MN Statutes Skip to main content Skip to office menu Skip to footer Minnesota Legislature Advanced Search Search Legislature Search Office of the Revisor of Statutes Office of the Revisor of Statutes 2024 Minnesota Statutes PROPERTY AND PROPERTY INTERESTS Chapter 513 Section 513.01 513.02 2024 Minnesota Statutes Authenticate PDF Resources Search Minnesota Statutes About Minnesota Statutes 2024 Statutes New, Amended or Repealed 2024 Table of Chapters 2024 Statutes Topics (Index) Chapter 513 Table of Sections Full Chapter Text Version List Section 513.01 Version List 513.01 NO ACTION ON AGREEMENT. No action shall be maintained, in either of the following cases, upon any agreement, unless such agreement, or some note or memorandum thereof, expressing the consideration, is in writing, and subscribed by the party charged therewith: (1) every agreement that by its terms is not to be performed within one year from the making thereof; (2) every special promise to answer for the debt, default or doings of another; (3) every agreement, promise, or undertaking made upon consideration of marriage, except mutual promises to marry; (4) every agreement, promise or undertaking to pay a debt which has been discharged by bankruptcy or insolvency proceedings. History: ( 8456 ) RL s 3483 Official Publication of the State of Minnesota Revisor of Statutes
perturbation.justification: The modified text, by allowing only oral communication and waiving written documentation, directly contradicts Minnesota Statute 513.01, which requires agreements not to be performed within one year to be in writing. The changed text eliminates this written requirement, making the agreement potentially unenforceable, as it removes a key element necessary for establishing a legally binding agreement under the Statute of Frauds. This aligns with the 'Inconsistencies Legal Contradiction' definition because the original contract (with indefinite term) could be interpreted to include a term longer than a year, in which case the changed text would cause the contract terms to violate state law.
